name: Lowest Common Multiple (LCM)

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 126 Q193 (real algebraic LCM problem, with answer key)
tnpscguru.in - TNPSC Aptitude LCM & HCF Questions & Answers
2017_q1 page 37 Q171 (algebraic HCF-from-LCM relationship, xy/z, with answer key, image-verified)

geeksforgeeks.org, indiabix.com - traffic-light/lighthouse cycle-coincidence variants, smallest-perfect-square-divisible-by-N problem shape

researchNotes: One direct real-exam hit (an unusually tricky algebraic LCM problem) plus 4 worked 'remainder' style LCM problems from a prep aggregator — remainder-type LCM problems ('least number which when divided by X, Y, Z leaves remainder R') are clearly a recurring TNPSC pattern given how many independent examples exist of this exact shape, so the reading material teaches that pattern explicitly as its own method, separate from plain 'find the LCM of these numbers'. Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only, also used Day 4): re-scanned OCR and found 2 more genuine hits, both image-verified (2017_q1 Q171's algebraic HCF-from-LCM identity, and 2024_q Q49's LCM-of-decimals problem) — plus a genuine tnpscguru HCF-LCM-with-sum/difference problem, and 4 authored extensions (ratio+LCM for 3 numbers, LCM of fractions, the classic 'bells tolling together' word problem, and the coprime-means-LCM-equals-product concept) to round out method coverage. Every new mock question carries a 'trick'; all arithmetic re-verified by script before finalizing. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): re-scanned OCR (no further genuine LCM hits beyond the original 2), then added problem-shape gaps: traffic-light/lighthouse cycle-coincidence variants (including a 'count how many times within a window' twist beyond simple 'when next'), the smallest-perfect-square-divisible-by-N shape, an LCM-is-k-times-HCF algebra shape, and one deliberate HCF-in-disguise question (m22) to test whether the reader can tell LCM and HCF word problems apart by their actual wording rather than by which topic file they're filed under. Roughly doubled mock questions (15→22); three arithmetic slips caught and corrected in early drafts of m16/m19/m20 before finalizing.

reading.intro: LCM is 1 of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows two distinct shapes: algebraic LCM-from-a-clue problems, and 'remainder' word problems — both are taught below as separate methods.

qa.question: Real exam (2025): If the LCM of 'a' and 'a+2' is 1300, find the LCM of 'a' and 'a+1'.
qa.answer: Since a and a+2 differ by 2, their HCF is either 1 or 2. If HCF=2: LCM = a(a+2)/2 = 1300 → a(a+2) = 2600. Solving a²+2a−2600=0 gives a=50 (check: 50×52/2 = 1300 ✓). Now find LCM(50, 51): consecutive integers are always coprime (HCF=1), so LCM = 50×51 = 2550.

qa.question: Method — 'least number leaving given remainders' (remainders differ from the divisors by a constant): Find the least number which, when divided by 16, 18, and 21, leaves remainders 3, 5, and 8 respectively.
qa.answer: Check the gap between each divisor and its remainder: 16−3=13, 18−5=13, 21−8=13 — all the same gap! When that happens, the answer is LCM(16,18,21) − 13. LCM(16,18,21) = 1008. Answer = 1008−13 = 995.

qa.question: Method — 'least number leaving the SAME remainder in every case': Find the least number which, when divided by 6, 7, 8, 9, and 12, leaves the same remainder 1 in every case.
qa.answer: When the remainder is identical across all divisors, the answer is LCM(divisors) + remainder. LCM(6,7,8,9,12) = 504. Answer = 504 + 1 = 505.

qa.question: Method — 'mixed condition' (a common remainder for some divisors, but exact division for another): Find the least number which, when divided by 5, 6, 7, and 8, leaves remainder 3 in each case, but leaves NO remainder when divided by 9.
qa.answer: First find the least number leaving remainder 3 for 5,6,7,8: that's LCM(5,6,7,8)+3 = 840+3 = 843. But we also need it divisible by 9. Keep adding LCM(5,6,7,8)=840 repeatedly to 843 until the result is divisible by 9: 843 (no), 1683 (1683÷9=187, yes!). Answer = 1683.

qa.question: Method — LCM of numbers given as prime factorizations: Find the LCM of (2×3×5×7) and (3×5×7×11).
qa.answer: LCM takes the HIGHEST power of every prime that appears in either number. Primes involved: 2,3,5,7,11 — each appears at most once in either factorization, so LCM = 2×3×5×7×11 (just the union of all prime factors, since none repeat).

qa.question: Real exam (2017_q1 Q171): If the LCM of x and y is z, what is the HCF of x and y?
qa.answer: Using the universal relationship HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers: HCF = (x×y) ÷ LCM = xy/z. This is the algebraic form of the same HCF×LCM=product rule used throughout HCF and LCM problems.

qa.question: Real exam (2024_q page 33 Q49): Find the LCM of 3, 2.7, and 0.09.
qa.answer: Multiply every number by the same power of 10 to clear all decimals: ×100 gives 300, 270, and 9. Find LCM(300,270,9): 300=2²×3×5², 270=2×3³×5, 9=3². LCM = 2²×3³×5² = 4×27×25 = 2,700. Now divide back by the same 100: 2,700÷100 = 27. So LCM(3, 2.7, 0.09) = 27.
qa.tip: For LCM of decimals: multiply all numbers by the same power of 10 to make them whole numbers first (using enough zeros for the number with the most decimal places), find the LCM of those whole numbers, then divide the result back by the same power of 10.

qa.question: Real-pattern problem (web-sourced, tnpscguru): The LCM and HCF of two numbers are 495 and 5 respectively. If their sum is 100, find their difference.
qa.answer: Numbers = 5m, 5n with m,n coprime. LCM = 5mn = 495 → mn = 99. Coprime pairs multiplying to 99: (1,99) or (9,11). Testing sums: 5×(1+99)=500 (too big); 5×(9+11)=100 ✓. So the numbers are 45 and 55. Their difference = 55−45 = 10.

qa.question: Method — LCM directly from a ratio (extends the HCF-from-ratio shortcut to LCM): Three numbers are in the ratio 2:3:4 and their LCM is 240. Find the numbers.
qa.answer: Write the numbers as 2k, 3k, 4k. LCM(2,3,4)=12, so LCM(2k,3k,4k)=12k. Set 12k=240 → k=20. The numbers are 2×20=40, 3×20=60, and 4×20=80.

qa.question: Method — LCM of fractions: Find the LCM of 2/3, 4/9, and 6/27.
qa.answer: LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators) ÷ HCF(denominators). LCM(2,4,6)=12. HCF(3,9,27)=3. LCM = 12/3 = 4. (Quick check: 4÷(2/3)=6, 4÷(4/9)=9, 4÷(6/27)=18 — all whole numbers, confirming 4 is a valid common multiple.)

qa.question: Real-pattern application: Three temple bells toll at intervals of 12, 18, and 24 minutes respectively. If all three toll together at 6:00 AM, when will they next toll together?
qa.answer: The next simultaneous toll happens after LCM(12,18,24) minutes. LCM(12,18)=36; LCM(36,24)=72. So they next toll together after 72 minutes = 1 hour 12 minutes → at 7:12 AM.
qa.tip: Any 'when will these repeating events coincide again' word problem (bells, traffic lights, rotating gears) is an LCM question — never HCF. HCF answers 'largest measuring unit'; LCM answers 'when do cycles line up again'.

qa.question: Concept check: When is the LCM of two numbers simply equal to their product?
qa.answer: Exactly when the two numbers are coprime (HCF=1) — since HCF×LCM=product always holds, LCM=product only when HCF=1. This is why consecutive integers (always coprime) have LCM = their product, as noted in r05 of the earlier set.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — Method: 'traffic lights' variant of the bell-tolling problem, with an added starting-time twist: Three traffic lights change every 48, 72, and 108 seconds respectively. If all three change together at 8:20:00 AM, at what time will they next change together?
qa.answer: Find LCM(48,72,108): 48=2⁴×3, 72=2³×3², 108=2²×3³. LCM = 2⁴×3³ = 16×27 = 432 seconds = 7 minutes 12 seconds. Next simultaneous change = 8:20:00 + 7:12 = 8:27:12 AM.

qa.question: Method — 'minimum tank-filling/tap' style LCM word problem: Three pipes fill a tank alone in 20, 25, and 30 minutes respectively. If all three start together, after how many minutes will they all next reach the SAME fill-cycle point together (i.e. all complete a whole number of their own fill cycles simultaneously)?
qa.answer: This is asking for LCM(20,25,30). 20=2²×5, 25=5², 30=2×3×5. LCM = 2²×3×5² = 300 minutes. (Note: this is a cycle-synchronization question, not a combined-work-rate question — combined tank-filling RATE problems belong to Time & Work, not LCM.)
qa.tip: Watch the question wording carefully: 'when do these repeating cycles next align' is LCM; 'how long together to complete one job' is a Time & Work rate problem — the same pipes/taps scenario can be dressed up as either type.

qa.question: Method — LCM used to find the smallest number that is simultaneously divisible by several given numbers AND satisfies an extra 'perfect square' condition: Find the smallest perfect square number that is exactly divisible by 8, 12, and 15.
qa.answer: First find LCM(8,12,15): 8=2³, 12=2²×3, 15=3×5. LCM=2³×3×5=120=2³×3¹×5¹. For a perfect square, every prime's exponent must be even — 2³ needs one more 2 (→2⁴), 3¹ needs one more 3 (→3²), 5¹ needs one more 5 (→5²). Multiply 120 by (2×3×5)=30: 120×30=3,600, which is 60² — a perfect square exactly divisible by 8, 12, and 15.
qa.tip: For 'smallest perfect square divisible by these numbers', find the LCM first, then multiply by whatever extra primes are needed to make every prime's exponent even.

questions.prompt: If the LCM of 'b' and 'b+2' is 180, and their HCF is 2, find the LCM of 'b' and 'b+1'.
questions.explanation: LCM = b(b+2)/HCF = 180 → b(b+2) = 360 → b²+2b−360=0 → b=18 (check: 18×20/2=180 ✓). Now LCM(18,19): consecutive integers are coprime, so LCM = 18×19 = 342.

questions.prompt: Find the least number which, when divided by 12, 15, and 20, leaves remainders 7, 10, and 15 respectively.
Find the least number which, when divided by 4, 5, and 6, leaves the same remainder 2 in each case.
Find the LCM of (2×2×3×5) and (2×3×3×7).
questions.explanation: Take the highest power of each prime: 2² (from the first), 3² (from the second), 5¹, 7¹. LCM = 2²×3²×5×7, which equals option (A) written out as 2×2×3×3×5×7.

questions.prompt: The LCM of two consecutive integers n and n+1 is always:
questions.explanation: Consecutive integers are always coprime (HCF=1), so their LCM is simply their product, n(n+1).

questions.prompt: If the LCM of x and y is z, and their HCF is h, which relationship always holds?
questions.explanation: HCF × LCM always equals the product of the two numbers: xy = hz.
questions.trick: Memorize 'product of numbers = HCF × LCM' as a single reusable identity — it solves most 'find the other number/HCF/LCM' problems instantly.

questions.prompt: Find the LCM of 4, 3.6, and 0.12.
questions.explanation: ×100 to clear decimals: 400, 360, 12. LCM(400,360,12)=3600. Divide back by 100: LCM = 36.
questions.trick: Multiply all numbers by the same power of 10 to clear decimals, find the integer LCM, then divide back by that same power of 10.

questions.prompt: The HCF and LCM of two numbers are 4 and 48 respectively. If their sum is 52, find their difference.
questions.explanation: Numbers = 4m, 4n, mn=(4×48)/16=12. Coprime pair (1,12) gives numbers 4 and 48 (sum=52 ✓). Difference = 48−4 = 44.
questions.trick: List coprime factor pairs of (HCF×LCM)/HCF² and test each against the given sum/difference clue.

questions.prompt: Three numbers are in the ratio 3:4:5 and their LCM is 1,800. Find the largest number.
questions.trick: Find LCM of the ratio terms themselves first (here, 60), then solve 60k = given LCM for k.

questions.prompt: Find the LCM of 1/3, 2/9, and 4/27.
questions.explanation: LCM(numerators 1,2,4)=4. HCF(denominators 3,9,27)=3. LCM = 4/3.
questions.trick: LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators) ÷ HCF(denominators) — the mirror image of the HCF-of-fractions formula.

questions.prompt: Three bells toll together, then toll at intervals of 15, 20, and 30 seconds respectively. After how many seconds will they next toll together?
questions.explanation: LCM(15,20,30) = 60 seconds.
questions.trick: 'When do repeating cycles next coincide' is always an LCM question.

questions.prompt: The LCM of two coprime numbers 15 and 22 is:
questions.explanation: Coprime numbers have LCM = their product: 15×22 = 330.
questions.trick: Coprime (HCF=1) numbers always have LCM = their straight product — no factorization needed.

questions.prompt: Find the LCM of (2²×3×5) and (2×3²×7).
questions.explanation: Take the highest power of each prime: 2² (from the first), 3² (from the second), 5¹, 7¹. LCM = 2²×3²×5×7 = 1,260.
questions.trick: For prime-factorized numbers, LCM always takes the HIGHEST power of each prime seen anywhere; HCF takes the LOWEST power common to all.

questions.prompt: The LCM and HCF of two numbers are 180 and 6 respectively. If one number is 30, find the other.
questions.explanation: Product = HCF×LCM = 6×180 = 1,080. Other number = 1,080÷30 = 36.
questions.trick: product = HCF × LCM, then divide by the known number to get the other.

questions.prompt: Find the least number which, when increased by 5, is exactly divisible by 12, 15, 18, and 27.
questions.explanation: LCM(12,15,18,27) = 540. The least number itself is 540−5 = 535 (so that adding 5 gives exactly 540).
questions.trick: For 'X more/less than a multiple of these divisors' problems, find the LCM first, then add or subtract the stated offset — don't add the offset before computing the LCM.

questions.prompt: Three traffic lights change every 36, 60, and 90 seconds respectively. If all three change together at a given moment, after how many minutes will they next change together?
questions.trick: Any 'traffic lights/bells changing together again' problem is LCM of the individual cycle lengths, converted to convenient time units at the end.

questions.prompt: Find the smallest perfect square number that is exactly divisible by 6, 9, and 15.
questions.explanation: LCM(6,9,15): 6=2×3, 9=3², 15=3×5. LCM=2×3²×5=90=2¹×3²×5¹. To make every exponent even, multiply by (2×5)=10: 90×10=900=30².
questions.trick: Find the LCM first, then multiply by whichever primes currently have an odd exponent (raising each to the next even power) to reach the smallest perfect square.

questions.prompt: Find the smallest perfect square number exactly divisible by 8 and 12.
questions.explanation: LCM(8,12)=24=2³×3. To make both exponents even, multiply by (2×3)=6: 24×6=144=12².
questions.trick: Same rule: LCM first, then multiply by the primes with odd exponents to reach a perfect square.

questions.prompt: Two lighthouses flash every 15 seconds and every 40 seconds respectively. If they flash together at midnight, how many times will they flash together in the next 10 minutes (600 seconds), not counting the initial flash?
questions.explanation: LCM(15,40)=120 seconds. In 600 seconds, they coincide every 120 seconds: 600/120 = 5 times after the start (at 120, 240, 360, 480, 600 seconds).
questions.trick: Count coincidences as (total time) ÷ LCM — the initial simultaneous flash at time zero is usually excluded from the count unless the question says otherwise.

questions.prompt: Find the LCM of 2/5, 4/15, and 6/25.
questions.explanation: LCM(numerators 2,4,6)=12. HCF(denominators 5,15,25)=5. LCM of the fractions = 12/5.
questions.trick: LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators)/HCF(denominators) — recompute the HCF of denominators carefully, it's easy to instead (wrongly) take their LCM.

questions.prompt: The LCM of two numbers is 5 times their HCF. If HCF + LCM = 72, find the HCF.
questions.explanation: Let HCF=h. LCM=5h. h+5h=72 → 6h=72 → h=12.
questions.trick: Translate 'LCM is k times HCF' directly into LCM=k×HCF, then solve the resulting single-variable equation.

questions.prompt: Find the least number of square tiles required to exactly pave a rectangular hall, given the tile side must be chosen so a WHOLE number of tiles fits along both a 6 m and a 9 m wall with the smallest possible tile count (largest tile size satisfying this).
questions.explanation: This is an HCF question in disguise (largest tile ⇒ fewest tiles): HCF(6,9)=3 m tile side. Tiles along 6 m wall = 2, along 9 m wall = 3, total = 2×3 = 6 tiles.
questions.trick: Don't apply LCM reflexively just because this topic is 'LCM' — 'largest tile / fewest pieces' phrasing is always HCF; only 'smallest number divisible by all' or 'cycles re-aligning' phrasing is LCM. Reading the question's exact ask matters more than which topic file it's filed under.
